title.emphasis: Part 2: The Threshold Analysis – Is the Instrument a Security?

section.para: Based on the characterization above, apply the appropriate Supreme Court test.
The Reves test determines if a note bears a "family resemblance" to an investment, thus classifying it as a security.

listitem.para: Motivations of Seller/Buyer: Is the cooperative raising money for general business use (leans toward security) or to facilitate a specific patron's transaction (leans away)? Is the buyer motivated by profit (leans toward security) or acquiring a good/service (leans away)?
Plan of Distribution: Is the note offered on a public market (leans toward security) or is the distribution narrowly limited to patrons for whom it facilitates a transaction (leans away)?
Reasonable Expectations of the Public: Is the instrument marketed as an "investment" (leans toward security) or as "patronage capital" or a "membership deposit" (leans away)?

Presence of an Alternative Regulatory Scheme: Does the framework of cooperative law and Subchapter T itself provide sufficient protection, making securities law unnecessary (leans away from security)?

section.para: The Howey test defines an investment contract as a security if these four conditions are met:

listitem.para: An Investment of Value: Has the holder given up something of value (money, assets, labor) to acquire the instrument?
In a Common Enterprise: Is the holder's fortune interwoven with the fortune of the cooperative and other members?
With an Expectation of Benefit/Profit: Does the holder expect to receive a valuable benefit, such as appreciation, dividends, or access to services?
Derived from the Efforts of Others: Does the realization of the benefit depend primarily on the managerial or operational efforts of the cooperative's leadership?
